assertion_results.text: The plan explicitly references reusing common/src/db/query.rs::apply_filter for comma-separated multi-value query parameter parsing rather than writing new parsing logic (constraint 5.4)
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md line 129: "Call `apply_filter(query, "license", license_value)` — this function already handles comma-separated parsing and IN clause generation". reuse-analysis.md Reuse Candidate 1 states: "The `apply_filter` function handles comma-separated multi-value query parameter parsing and SQL IN clause generation" and "This eliminates the need to write any custom comma-parsing or IN clause generation logic."

assertion_results.text: The plan references following the filter pattern from modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs as a structural guide for implementing the license filter
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md line 108: "Add an `Option<String>` field named `license` to the existing `Query` struct (following the same pattern as the `severity` field in `modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs`)". plan.md line 44-48: lists the advisory list endpoint as a sibling pattern / Reuse Candidate and instructs to "understand how it extracts and applies the severity filter — this is the structural template for the license filter". reuse-analysis.md Reuse Candidate 2 explicitly states: "The advisory handler's pattern is used as a structural template (not copied verbatim) for modifying the package list handler."

assertion_results.text: The plan references using entity/src/package_license.rs for the JOIN query rather than writing raw SQL or creating a new entity
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md lines 125-126: "Join through the `package_license` entity (`entity/src/package_license.rs`) to filter packages by their associated license SPDX identifiers". plan.md line 131: "Use the `package_license` SeaORM entity for the JOIN". reuse-analysis.md Reuse Candidate 3: "Used in `PackageService::list()` to construct the JOIN query when the license filter is active. Instead of writing raw SQL, the implementation uses SeaORM's relation model"

assertion_results.text: The reuse analysis covers all 3 Reuse Candidates from the task description, explaining how each would be applied
assertion_results.evidence: reuse-analysis.md contains three detailed sections: "Reuse Candidate 1: `common/src/db/query.rs::apply_filter`" (lines 10-36), "Reuse Candidate 2: `modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs`" (lines 38-67), and "Reuse Candidate 3: `entity/src/package_license.rs`" (lines 69-96). Each section includes "What it provides", "How it will be reused" with code examples or specific steps, "Reuse type", and "Benefit" subsections.

assertion_results.text: All files listed in the plan are within the scope defined by the task's Files to Modify and Files to Create sections (constraint 5.1)
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md Task Description Parsing (lines 25-26) identifies Files to Modify as: `modules/fundamental/src/package/endpoints/list.rs`, `modules/fundamental/src/package/service/mod.rs`, and Files to Create as: `tests/api/package_license_filter.rs`. The plan's "Files to Modify" section covers exactly those 2 files (sections 1 and 2). Section 3 mentions `modules/fundamental/src/package/endpoints/mod.rs` but only as a potential verification target ("No change expected, but verify during code understanding"). The "Files to Create" section lists only `tests/api/package_license_filter.rs`. The Self-Verification Plan (line 196-197) acknowledges `tests/api/mod.rs` may need updating for module registration but explicitly calls it out as "an expected out-of-scope file that is necessary for the test to compile". No other files are proposed for modification or creation.

assertion_results.text: The plan does not propose creating new utility functions that duplicate the functionality of apply_filter (constraint 5.4)
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md line 129 explicitly states to call apply_filter directly: "Call `apply_filter(query, "license", license_value)`". reuse-analysis.md states: "No new utility code, helper functions, or shared modules need to be created — the existing infrastructure fully supports the license filter feature" (lines 109-110). No section of the plan proposes writing custom parsing, splitting, or IN clause generation logic.
